1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/DualInverse.lean
7	
8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
131	avoids. The
132	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
133	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
134	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
135	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
136	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
137	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
138	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
139	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
140	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
141	
142	\section{Mathlib API survey}
143	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
144	
145	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
146	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
147	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
148	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
149	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
150	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
151	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
152	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
153	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
154	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
155	source.
156	
157	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
158	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
159	monoidal-category instance
160	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
161	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
162	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
163	\[
164	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
165	\]
166	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
167	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
168	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
169	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
170	% SOURCE QUOTE:
171	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
172	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
173	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
174	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
175	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
176	%   ...
177	%   tensorUnit := unit _
178	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
179	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
180	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
181	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct} /
182	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in
183	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}, L104--L125.}
184	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
185	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
186	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
187	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
188	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
189	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
190	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
191	
192	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's

1	\chapter{The sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence (shared slice root)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SheafOverEquivalence.lean
4	
5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	
10	\leanok
11	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
12	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
14	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
15	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
16	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
17	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
18	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
19	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
20	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
21	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
22	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
23	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
24	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
25	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
26	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
27	  equivalence
28	  \[
29	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
30	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
31	  \]
32	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
33	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
34	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
35	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
36	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
37	  \[
38	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
40	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
41	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
42	  \]
43	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
44	  obtained by instantiating
45	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
46	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
47	  \[
48	    \varphi \;:\;
49	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
50	      \;\longrightarrow\;
51	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
52	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
53	  \]
54	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
55	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
56	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
57	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
58	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\begin{proof}
62	
63	\leanok
64	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
65	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
66	
67	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
68	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
69	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
70	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
71	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
72	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
73	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
74	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
75	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
76	  is discharged for this case.
77	
78	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
79	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
80	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
81	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
82	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
83	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
84	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
85	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
86	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
87	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
88	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
89	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
90	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
91	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
92	
93	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
94	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
95	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
96	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
97	\end{proof}
98	
99	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
100	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}
103	
104	\leanok
105	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
106	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
108	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
109	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
110	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
111	  \[
112	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
113	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
114	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
115	  \]
116	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
117	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
118	\end{lemma}
119	
120	\begin{proof}
121	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
122	  \leanok
123	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
124	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
125	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
126	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
127	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
128	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
129	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
130	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
131	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
132	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
133	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
134	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
135	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
136	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
137	\end{proof}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}
140	
141	\leanok
142	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
143	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
145	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
146	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
147	  a canonical isomorphism
148	  \[
149	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
150	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
151	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
152	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
155	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
156	\end{lemma}
157	
158	\begin{proof}
159	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
160	  \leanok
161	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
162	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
163	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
164	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
165	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
166	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
167	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
168	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
169	  genuine isomorphism.
170	
171	  Concretely, the canonical comparison map realising this transport is the
172	  unit-to-pushforward-of-unit morphism
173	  \[
174	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi
175	      \;:\;
176	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)
177	      \;\longrightarrow\;
178	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,
179	        \bigl(\mathtt{unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr).
180	  \]
181	  Its section at an open \(W\) is exactly the section-level ring map
182	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\): the morphism acts on a section by multiplication
183	  through \(\varphi\), so on \(W\) it is the additive-group map underlying
184	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) (the characterisation
185	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}\)). Since \(\varphi\) is an
186	  isomorphism of ring sheaves, each component \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) is a ring
187	  isomorphism, hence its underlying additive-group map is an isomorphism.
188	
189	  It follows that \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
190	  One reflects the sectionwise isomorphism upward through the two forgetful
191	  functors --- first \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) (down to presheaves of
192	  modules), then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf}\) (down to presheaves of
193	  additive groups) --- so that being an isomorphism reduces to the componentwise
194	  isomorphism check
195	  \(\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}\) on the underlying natural
196	  transformation, which is the sectionwise statement just established. The desired
197	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) is then the inverse of the resulting
198	  isomorphism, \((\mathtt{asIso}\,(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi))^{-1}\).
199	  This is the slice-site analogue of
200	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).
201	\end{proof}
202	
203	\section{The engine corollary}
204	\label{sec:soe_chart}
205	
206	\begin{lemma}
207	
208	\leanok
209	[Over--restrict trivialisation bridge]
210	  \label{lem:chart_over_iso}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso}
212	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
213	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) open, and suppose
214	  given a trivialisation of the restriction along the open immersion
215	  \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\),
216	  \[
217	    e \;:\; M|_\iota \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
218	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.
219	  \]
220	  Then there is a canonical isomorphism in the slice category
221	  \[
222	    \mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e \;:\;
223	      M.\mathtt{over}\,U \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
224	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
225	  \]
226	\end{lemma}
227	
228	\begin{proof}
229	  \uses{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso, lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso,
230	        def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
231	        \leanok
232	  Transport the trivialisation \(e\) across the over-equivalence and reconcile the
233	  two ends with the consumer isomorphisms. Explicitly, \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\,M\,U\,e\)
234	  is the composite
235	  \[
236	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U
237	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ (\mathtt{restrictOverIso})^{-1}\ }
238	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
239	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\ }
240	      \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{unit}\,\cdots)
241	      \xrightarrow[\sim]{\ \mathtt{unitOverIso}\ }
242	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr).
243	  \]
244	  The first arrow is the inverse of \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}; the
245	  middle arrow is the image of \(e\) under the (functor of the) over-equivalence,
246	  which is functorial in isomorphisms hence sends the iso \(e\) to an iso; the last
247	  arrow is \cref{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}. Each factor is an isomorphism,
248	  so the composite is.
249	
250	  This lemma addresses the site mismatch in the chart-presentation of
251	  \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}: the given trivialisation \(e\) lives in
252	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) (the open
253	  subspace site), whereas the finite-presentation transport
254	  \(\mathtt{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) must act in
255	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U)\) (the slice
256	  site); \(\mathtt{chartOverIso}\) crosses that gap. It is stated in full
257	  generality in \(M\), \(U\), \(e\) for use in
258	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}. The over-equivalence of
259	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} likewise underlies the internal-Hom
260	  theory: the commutativity of the internal Hom with the slice-site change of base
261	  (\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}) passes through the same equivalence.
262	\end{proof}

242	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
243	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
244	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
245	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
246	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
247	\(A\).
248	
249	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
250	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
251	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
252	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
253	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
254	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
255	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
256	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
257	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
258	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
259	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
260	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
261	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
262	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
263	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
264	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
265	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
266	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
267	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
268	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
269	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
270	
271	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
272	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
273	is already present in Mathlib: the file
274	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
275	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
276	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
277	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
278	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
279	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
280	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
281	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
282	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
283	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
284	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
285	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
286	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
287	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
288	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
289	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
290	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
291	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
292	
293	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
294	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
295	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
296	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
297	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
298	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
299	independently of any upstream timing.

301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
401	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
402	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
403	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
404	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
405	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
406	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
407	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
408	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
409	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
410	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
411	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
412	
413	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
414	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
415	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
416	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
418	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
419	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
420	  fixed-base presheaves in
421	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
423	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
424	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
425	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
426	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
427	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
428	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
429	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
430	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
431	
432	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
433	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
434	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
435	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
436	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
437	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
438	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
439	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
440	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
441	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
442	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
443	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
444	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
446	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
447	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
448	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
449	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
450	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
451	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
452	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
453	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
454	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
455	\end{remark}

480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).

1	\chapter{Coherence of locally trivial line bundles}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundleCoherence}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundleCoherence.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter supplies the cheap, on-path half of the
6	% line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme embedding
7	% (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}).
8	% The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a
9	% line bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a
10	% finitely presented \(\mathcal{O}\)-module. The embedding's input is a point of
11	% the relative Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), whose carrier is the
12	% \emph{locally trivial} predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) of
13	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (the project-side stand-in for an
14	% invertible sheaf, a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules locally isomorphic to
15	% \(\mathcal{O}\) of rank one). The chapter proves
16	% \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial} M \Rightarrow M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\),
17	% together with a rank-one / flatness record. The general implication
18	% ``\(M\) tensor-invertible \(\Rightarrow\) \(M\) locally free of rank one''
19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is
20	% \emph{not} crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
21	% finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55	
56	\section{Site instances for quasi-coherent data}
57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /
60	% QuasicoherentData are stated under three site hypotheses on the slice
61	% topologies J.over X of the underlying site J of X.ringCatSheaf:
62	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
63	%   [∀ x, HasWeakSheafify (J.over x) AddCommGrpCat]
64	%   [∀ x, (J.over x).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
65	% These must be discharged (or assumed as instances) before the statements in
66	% this chapter typecheck for X.ringCatSheaf. The project already lives in the
67	% SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf world, so these should resolve; this is the one
68	% item to verify when the Lean file is scaffolded.
69	
70	The Mathlib predicates \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) and
71	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\) are stated relative to a
72	Grothendieck topology \(J\) on a base category \(C\) and a sheaf of rings
73	\(R : \Sheaf(J, \RingCat)\). They require, for every object \(x : C\), three
74	instances on the slice topology \(J/x\) (Mathlib \(J.\mathtt{over}\ x\)):
75	\[
76	  (J/x).\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}(\forget_{\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat}),\quad
77	  \HasWeakSheafify(J/x, \AddCommGrpCat),\quad
78	  (J/x).\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}(\AddCommGrpCat).
79	\]
80	For \(X.\mathtt{Modules} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\ X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
81	these are the ambient site instances of the ringed space underlying \(X\). We
82	record their availability as a standing assumption for the chapter; verifying it
83	for \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is the single instance-resolution check the
84	formalisation must clear at entry, and is independent of the mathematical
85	content below.
86	
87	\begin{remark}
88	  \label{rem:lbc_site_instances}
89	  For the ringed-space site underlying \(X\) with sheaf of rings
90	  \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), every slice topology \(J/x\) carries the
91	  three instances \(\mathtt{HasSheafCompose}\), \(\HasWeakSheafify\) and
92	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) (for the forgetful functor
93	  \(\RingCat \to \AddCommGrpCat\)) demanded by
94	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}\). Under this assumption the
95	  predicate \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) is well-formed for every
96	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\).
97	\end{remark}
98	
99	\section{From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover}
100	\label{sec:lbc_cover}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}\leanok
103	[Trivialising affine cover]
104	  \label{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover}
106	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
107	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
108	  % "invertible module" (Tag 01CS), Section 17.25 (Tag 01CR)
109	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4059).
110	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$
116	  %  \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
117	  %  \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
118	  %  $$
119	  %  is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
120	  %  {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
121	  %  to $\mathcal{O}_X$."
122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
127	  \[
128	    e_i \;:\; M|_{U_i} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \struct{U_i}
129	  \]
130	  to the structure sheaf-as-module on \(U_i\).
131	\end{lemma}
132	
133	\begin{proof}
134	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
135	  \leanok
136	  Apply the hypothesis \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\) at every point \(x \in X\): it
137	  produces an affine open \(U_x \ni x\) and an isomorphism
138	  \(e_x : M|_{U_x} \cong \struct{U_x}\). Take the index set \(I := X\) (the
139	  underlying set of points), and to the index \(x\) assign the chosen
140	  neighbourhood \(U_x\) and the chosen isomorphism \(e_x\). The family
141	  \(\{U_x\}_{x \in X}\) covers \(X\) because each \(x\) lies in its own \(U_x\).
142	  This is exactly the data the definition supplies, packaged as an indexed
143	  family rather than a pointwise existential.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\section{The trivial presentation on a chart}
147	\label{sec:lbc_chart_presentation}
148	
149	\begin{lemma}\leanok
150	[Finite free presentation on a chart]
151	  \label{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation}
153	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
154	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
155	  % "Modules of finite presentation" (\label{definition-finite-presentation})
156	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L1377-L1392).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "We say that $\mathcal{F}$ is of {\it finite presentation}
159	  %  if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an open neighbourhood
160	  %  $x\in U \subset X$, and  $n, m \in \mathbf{N}$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$
161	  %  is isomorphic to the cokernel of a map
162	  %  $$
163	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{j = 1, \ldots, m}
164	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
165	  %  \longrightarrow
166	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{i = 1, \ldots, n}
167	  %  \mathcal{O}_U
168	  %  $$"
169	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Modules of finite
170	  presentation''.}
171	  On each chart \(U_i\) of the trivialising cover of
172	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
173	  trivial finite free presentation
174	  \[
175	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
176	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
177	  \]
178	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
179	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
180	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
181	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
182	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
183	  this presentation is finite.
184	\end{lemma}
185	
186	\begin{proof}
187	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
188	  \leanok
189	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
190	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
191	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
192	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
193	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
194	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
195	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
196	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
197	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
198	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
199	
200	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
201	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
202	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
203	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
204	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
205	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
206	  % NOTE. The over-restrict categorical bridge is the shared construction
207	  % `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (lem:chart_over_iso,
208	  % chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
209	\end{proof}
210	
211	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
212	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
213	
214	\begin{theorem}\leanok
215	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
216	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
218	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
219	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
220	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
221	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
223	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
224	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
225	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
226	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
228	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
229	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
230	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
231	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
232	\end{theorem}
233	
234	\begin{proof}
235	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
236	  \leanok
237	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
238	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
239	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
240	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
241	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
242	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
243	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
244	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
245	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
246	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
247	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
248	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
249	
250	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
251	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
252	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
253	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
254	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
255	\end{proof}
256	
257	\begin{corollary}\leanok
258	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
259	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
261	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
262	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
263	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
264	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
265	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
266	\end{corollary}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
270	  \leanok
271	  Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
272	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation}\), immediate
273	  from \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}. Quasi-coherence is immediate from
274	  finite presentation by
275	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\section{Rank one and flatness record}
279	\label{sec:lbc_rank_flat}
280	
281	% NOTE. Mathlib (at the pin) has no SheafOfModules-level "locally free of rank r"
282	% or "flat" predicate (verified absent: no SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree). The
283	% rank-one and flatness facts are therefore recorded chart-locally, as the data
284	% the Quot embedding actually consumes, rather than as instances of a missing
285	% global predicate. Project-side predicates may be introduced if a downstream
286	% consumer needs them packaged; this is flagged for the plan agent.
287	
288	\begin{lemma}\leanok
289	[Chart-local rank one and flatness]
290	  \label{lem:lbc_rank_flat}
291	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one}
292	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
293	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, section
294	  % "Locally free sheaves" (\label{definition-locally-free}, "(finite)
295	  % locally free of rank $r$")
296	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2079-L2094).
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
298	  % "\item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it locally free} if for every
299	  %  point $x \in X$ there exist a set $I$ and an open
300	  %  neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$
301	  %  such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to
302	  %  $\bigoplus_{i \in I} \mathcal{O}_X|_U$ as an $\mathcal{O}_X|_U$-module.
303	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free} if we may
304	  %  choose the index sets $I$ to be finite.
305	  %  \item We say $\mathcal{F}$ is {\it finite locally free of rank $r$}
306	  %  if we may choose the index sets $I$ to have cardinality $r$."
307	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules, ``Locally free sheaves'' (finite
308	  locally free of rank \(r\)).}
309	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial. The lemma records, at each
310	  point \(x \in X\), a chart \(U \ni x\) together with the trivialising iso
311	  \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) (to \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\)). This iso is the
312	  rank-one free \emph{model}: rank-one freeness and flatness are
313	  \emph{consequences} of this iso, because \(\struct{U}\) is the free
314	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module of rank one and a flat module over itself (a ring is
315	  flat over itself), and both properties transport along \(e\). In the sense of
316	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} this exhibits \(M\) as finite locally free
317	  of rank one and flat on the cover.
318	\end{lemma}
319	
320	\begin{proof}
321	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
322	  \leanok
323	  Immediate from the trivialisation: on \(U_i\), \(e_i\) identifies \(M|_{U_i}\)
324	  with \(\struct{U_i} = \bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), the free module
325	  of rank one. The structure sheaf-as-module is flat over itself, and an
326	  isomorphic module is flat; the index set \(\{*\}\) has cardinality one, so the
327	  rank is one. The properties are recorded chart-locally because rank-one
328	  freeness and flatness are local conditions, and the chart-local data are what
329	  the Quot-scheme embedding consumes when it forms the Hilbert-polynomial input
330	  on a fibre.
331	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
79	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
80	
81	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
82	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
83	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
84	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
85	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
88	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
89	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
90	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
91	  %  [\dots]
92	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
93	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
94	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
95	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
96	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
97	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
98	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
99	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
100	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
101	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
102	  \[
103	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
104	  \]
105	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
106	  \[
107	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
108	  \]
109	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
110	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
111	  \[
112	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
113	  \]
114	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
115	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
116	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
117	\end{lemma}
118	
119	% NOTE (iter-247): the import cycle is RESOLVED. As of iter-247 the dependency flows
120	% LineBundlePullback -> TensorObjSubstrate -> RelPicFunctor (RelPicFunctor now imports
121	% TensorObjSubstrate), so the tensor substrate is upstream and its real decls are directly
122	% citable here: tensorObj, tensorObj_assoc_iso, tensorObj_comm/unit/left/right isos,
123	% tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial, exists_tensorObj_inverse, pullbackUnitIso, and (when it
124	% lands) the loc-triv comparison iso pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv. The four local
125	% pure-Mathlib bridge copies built in iter-246 (pTensor_isLocallyTrivial, pAssoc,
126	% isLocallyTrivial_unit, exists_pTensor_inverse) should now be rewired to those upstream
127	% proofs, collapsing the construction to a real AddCommGroup modulo only the genuine
128	% project-deferred sorry exists_tensorObj_inverse (and a typed bridge on the not-yet-landed
129	% comparison iso, used only for the pullback-additivity of Step 2).
130	% Carrier caveat (still live, unrelated to the cycle): the carrier setoid
131	% RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup (LineBundlePullback.lean) is the iso-class relation
132	% Nonempty(L ≅ L'), so the realized group is the tensor-product Picard group on loc-triv
133	% iso-classes (additive mirror of picCommGroup). The quotient-by-H_T of Steps 2-4 below is
134	% the eventual relative-Picard carrier; Step 1's loc-triv group is what the Lean instance
135	% realizes now. See memory rpf-import-cycle-blocks-tensorobj (now resolved) +
136	% rpf-addcommgroup-real-skeleton.
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
139	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
140	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
141	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
142	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
143	\begin{proof}
144	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
145	    thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
146	    lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
147	    lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
148	    lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
149	    lem:pullback_unit_iso, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}
150	
151	
152	\leanok
153	  The construction proceeds in four steps over the locally-trivial carrier
154	  \[
155	    \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) \;=\;
156	      \bigl\{\, M : \text{a module on } C \times_k T \;\bigm|\;
157	      M \text{ is locally trivial} \,\bigr\}
158	  \]
159	  of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, on whose isomorphism classes the
160	  relative Picard quotient is taken. It is authored \emph{in parallel} against
161	  two inputs being built concurrently: the locally-trivial pullback--tensor
162	  comparison isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} (consumed in
163	  Step~2 to make \(\pi_T^*\) additive) and the tensor inverse
164	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (consumed in Step~1 for group
165	  closure); both are themselves deferred targets.
166	
167	  \emph{Step 1 (abelian group on the carrier).} On isomorphism classes of the
168	  carrier \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T)\), addition is the descent of the
169	  scheme-level tensor product of line bundles,
170	  \[
171	    [L] + [L'] \;:=\; [\, L \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} L' \,],
172	  \]
173	  where \(L \otimes L'\) is the locally-trivial descent of the scheme-level
174	  tensor product (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}); the sum lands again in
175	  the carrier because the tensor product of two locally-trivial modules is
176	  locally trivial (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The zero
177	  element is the class \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\) of the structure sheaf,
178	  the monoidal unit, which is locally trivial. Associativity, commutativity,
179	  and left/right unitality of \(+\) descend from the scheme-level coherence
180	  isomorphisms --- the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the
181	  braiding (\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}), and the unitors
182	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) --- exactly as for the absolute Picard group
183	  on tensor-invertible isomorphism classes
184	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The additive inverse is
185	  \[
186	    -[L] \;:=\; [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \,],
187	  \]
188	  where \(L^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the witness supplied by
189	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: it returns a \emph{locally-trivial}
190	  module together with an isomorphism
191	  \(L \otimes L^{\mathrm{inv}} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\), so the inverse
192	  stays inside the loc-triv carrier and the group is closed under negation.
193	
194	  \emph{Step 2 (the pullback homomorphism \(\pi_T^*\)).} Pullback along the
195	  projection \(\pi_T\) gives an additive map
196	  \[
197	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T),
198	  \]
199	  whose underlying assignment of line bundles is
200	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}. It is a homomorphism of abelian groups:
201	  it sends the unit to the unit, since
202	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) by the unconditional
203	  pullback--unit isomorphism (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}); and it is additive,
204	  since pullback commutes with tensor product on locally-trivial modules,
205	  \[
206	    \pi_T^*(N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} N') \;\cong\;
207	      \pi_T^* N \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^* N',
208	  \]
209	  by the locally-trivial comparison isomorphism
210	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} specialised to the projection
211	  \(\pi_T\) on the \(\mathrm{OnProduct}\) carrier. This is the exact analogue,
212	  for the geometric pullback, of the additive monoid homomorphism on Picard
213	  groups induced by a ring map respecting tensor product, and is modelled on it
214	  field-for-field. Write \(H_T := \mathrm{im}(\pi_T^*)\) for its image, a
215	  subgroup of the carrier's isomorphism-class group.
216	
217	  \emph{Step 3 (setoid reconciliation).} The set-valued quotient of
218	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is taken modulo the
219	  isomorphism-class equivalence that declares two line bundles related when
220	  their classes differ by an element of \(H_T\). This relation coincides with
221	  the left-coset relation of the subgroup \(H_T\) inside the abelian group of
222	  Steps~1--2: both encode
223	  \[
224	    L \sim L' \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
225	      [\, L^{\mathrm{inv}} \otimes L' \,] \in H_T .
226	  \]
227	  Establishing this equivalence of relations is a concrete isomorphism-class
228	  argument over the carrier; it consumes the tensor inverse (Step~1) and the
229	  group law, but not the computational content of the comparison isomorphism.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 4 (transport).} By Step~3 the quotient set underlying
232	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is in canonical bijection with the group quotient
233	  \(\mathrm{OnProduct}(\pi_C, \pi_T) / H_T\), which carries the canonical
234	  abelian-group structure of a quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup.
235	  Transporting this structure along the bijection of Step~3 endows
236	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) with its abelian-group structure, under which the
237	  canonical surjection
238	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a
239	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
240	\end{proof}

242	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
243	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
244	
245	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
246	group-valued presheaf.

427	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
428	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
429	
430	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
431	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
432	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
433	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
434	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
435	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
436	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
437	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
438	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

610	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
611	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
612	
613	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
614	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
615	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
616	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
617	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
618	  % property)
619	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
620	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
621	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
622	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
623	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
624	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
625	  % topologies  by
626	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
627	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
628	  %  \end{dfn}
629	  %  [\dots]
630	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
631	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
632	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
633	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
634	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
635	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
636	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
637	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
638	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
639	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
640	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
641	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
642	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
643	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
644	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
645	  \[
646	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
647	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
648	  \]
649	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
650	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
651	  of presheaves of abelian groups
652	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
653	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
654	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
655	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
656	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
657	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
658	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
659	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
660	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
661	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
662	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
663	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
664	  \(\eta\).
665	\end{theorem}
666	
667	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
668	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
669	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
670	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
671	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
672	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
673	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
674	\begin{proof}
675	
676	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
677	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
678	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
679	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
680	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
681	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
682	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
683	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
684	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
685	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
686	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
687	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
688	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
689	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
690	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
691	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
692	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
693	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
694	\end{proof}

7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
