29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54

19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires

49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\

3866	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural,
3867	        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
3868	  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
3869	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} =
3870	  \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) for the sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison of
3871	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}, \(h^{*}(-) = (\mathtt{pullback}\,h).\mathrm{map}\) for
3872	  the pullback functor, and \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) for the pullback
3873	  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism \((h \circ f)^{*} \cong h^{*}\circ f^{*}\). Then the
3874	  comparison for the composite \(h \circ f\) factors through the comparisons for \(f\)
3875	  and for \(h\), conjugated by \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\):
3876	  \[
3877	    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,h \circ f}(M, N)
3878	      \;=\;
3879	      (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}_{M \otimes_X N} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3880	      h^{*}\!\bigl(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,f}(M, N)\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3881	      \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,h}(f^{*}M,\, f^{*}N) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3882	      \mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M,\,
3883	        (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_N\bigr).\mathrm{hom},
3884	  \]
3885	  an equality of morphisms \((h \circ f)^{*}(M \otimes_X N) \to (h^{*}f^{*}M)
3886	  \otimes_Z (h^{*}f^{*}N)\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,Z\), where
3887	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) is the functoriality of \(\otimes\) in a pair of
3888	  isomorphisms.
3889	
3890	  \emph{Remark (the open-immersion base-change square).} The base-change form consumed
3891	  downstream by \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} is the \emph{specialisation} of
3892	  this coherence. Given \(f : Y \to X\), an open \(U \subseteq X\) with preimage
3893	  \(f^{-1}U\), the restriction \(g : f^{-1}U \to U\) and the open immersions
3894	  \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\), \(j' : f^{-1}U \hookrightarrow Y\), the morphism
3895	  \(f^{-1}U \to X\) admits the two equal factorisations \(j' \circ f = g \circ j\)
3896	  (i.e. \(j' \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} j\)). Applying the displayed coherence to
3897	  each factorisation and equating the two results identifies the restriction
3898	  \((j')^{*}\bigl(\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\bigr)\) with
3899	  \(\delta^{g}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j^{*}M, j^{*}N)\), through the pseudofunctoriality
3900	  isomorphisms; in particular each is an isomorphism if and only if the other is. This
3901	  is the form used in the chart-chase, where \(M, N\) are trivialised on \(U\).
3902	\end{lemma}
3903	
3904	\begin{proof}
3905	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural,
3906	        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
3907	        lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
3908	  Unfolding \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\)
3909	  (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) on both sides exposes each as the same four-fold
3910	  composite
3911	  \[
3912	    S_1 \;\mathbin{;}\; a.\mathrm{map}\,\delta \;\mathbin{;}\; S_3 \;\mathbin{;}\; S_4,
3913	  \]
3914	  where \(S_1\) is the sheafification--pullback comparison
3915	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\), \(a.\mathrm{map}\,\delta\) is the
3916	  sheafification of the presheaf-level oplax comparison \(\delta\) of
3917	  \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}, \(S_3\) is the monoidal-unit
3918	  sheafification isomorphism \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), and \(S_4\) is the
3919	  tensor of the connecting isomorphisms \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\). The identity to
3920	  prove is therefore a paste of \emph{four commuting squares}, conjugated throughout by
3921	  the pullback pseudofunctoriality isomorphism \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\). Unlike
3922	  the \emph{naturality} paste of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural} (D1$'$), this is
3923	  a \emph{composition}-coherence paste: the left-hand side is the composite-morphism
3924	  (\(h \circ f\)) instance, while the right-hand side interleaves the \(f\)-instance
3925	  (transported by \(h^{*}\)) with the \(h\)-instance (on the pulled-back modules
3926	  \(f^{*}M, f^{*}N\)).
3927	
3928	  We record \emph{why} the unit-comparison mirror does not apply. The unit coherence
3929	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} succeeds because
3930	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is, by definition, an adjunction transpose, so its
3931	  composition coherence is obtained by transposing across the pullback--pushforward
3932	  adjunction. The tensorator \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) is \emph{not} a transpose ---
3933	  it is the four-fold composite above --- and there is no analogous
3934	  \(\mathtt{homEquiv}\)-bridge, so the transpose-and-inject opening of the unit analog
3935	  leaves an un-evaluable transpose of a concrete composite. The proof instead
3936	  pastes the four squares directly.
3937	
3938	  \emph{Sq2 (the \(\delta\) core).} The presheaf-level oplax comparison \(\delta\) of the
3939	  \emph{composite} pullback decomposes by the Mathlib oplax-monoidal coherence
3940	  \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\_}\delta\), once the composite presheaf pullback
3941	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h \circ f}\) is identified with the composite functor
3942	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f} \,\circ\, \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h}\) through the
3943	  Mathlib presheaf coherence \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\). This
3944	  identification rests on the ring-map reconciliation
3945	  \[
3946	    (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,(h \circ f)).\mathrm{hom}
3947	      \;=\; \varphi'_{f} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3948	      (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base})^{\mathrm{op}}.\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\,\varphi'_{h},
3949	  \]
3950	  which is \emph{definitional}: although the two sides are presented in functor categories
3951	  that nominally differ by the equality \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,(h.\mathrm{base} \circ
3952	  f.\mathrm{base}) = \mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base} \,\circ\, \mathtt{Opens.map}\,
3953	  h.\mathrm{base}\) (\(\mathtt{Opens.map\_comp}\)), this equality and the underlying
3954	  scheme-composition data already hold up to definitional equality at default
3955	  transparency, so the reconciliation is definitional
3956	  (\(\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom\_comp\_hom\_reconcile}\)). No pseudofunctor-coherence
3957	  transport and none of the associativity/unit bookkeeping atoms
3958	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.}\{\mathtt{pullbackId}, \mathtt{pullback\_assoc},
3959	  \mathtt{pullback\_comp\_id}, \mathtt{pullback\_id\_comp}\}\) are required for this

5600	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
5601	  \[
5602	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
5603	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
5604	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
5605	  \]
5606	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
5607	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
5608	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
5609	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
5610	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
5611	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
5612	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
5613	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
5614	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
5615	\end{lemma}

5617	\begin{proof}
5618	
5619	\leanok
5620	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
5621	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
5622	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
5623	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
5624	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
5625	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
5626	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
5627	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
5628	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
5629	  \(\iota\)).
5630	
5631	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
5632	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
5633	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
5634	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
5635	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
5636	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
5637	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
5638	  identification
5639	  \[
5640	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
5641	    \qquad (V \le U),
5642	  \]
5643	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
5644	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
5645	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
5646	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
5647	\end{proof}

5654	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
5655	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
5656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
5657	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5658	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
5659	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
5660	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
5661	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5662	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
5663	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
5664	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
5665	  % then the canonical map
5666	  % $$
5667	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
5668	  % \longrightarrow
5669	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
5670	  % $$
5671	  % is an isomorphism."
5672	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
5673	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
5674	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
5675	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5676	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
5677	  \[
5678	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
5679	  \]
5680	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
5681	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
5682	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
5683	\end{lemma}
5684	
5685	\begin{proof}
5686	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5687	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5688	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5689	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5690	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5691	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5692	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5693	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5694	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5695	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5696	
5697	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5698	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5699	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5700	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5701	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5702	  \[
5703	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5704	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5705	  \]
5706	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5707	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5708	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5709	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
5710	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
5711	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
5712	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
5713	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5714	
5715	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
5716	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
5717	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
5718	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
5719	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
5720	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
5721	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
5722	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
5723	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
5724	  \emph{exactly}
5725	  \[
5726	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
5727	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5728	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
5729	  \]
5730	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
5731	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
5732	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
5733	  two-leg split of the whole step.
5734	
5735	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5736	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5737	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5738	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
5739	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
5740	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
5741	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
5742	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
5743	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
5744	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5745	
5746	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5747	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5748	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5749	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5750	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5751	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5752	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5753	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5754	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5755	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5756	
5757	  \emph{The leg-(A) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Leg (A) is realised by a single
5758	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\), built
5759	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
5760	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
5761	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
5762	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
5763	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
5764	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
5765	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
5766	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). Leg (A) must
5767	  therefore be constructed directly.
5768	
5769	  Concretely, \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) is the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation
5770	  of the slice-Hom components across the open-immersion functor \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\),
5771	  transported along the down-set identity \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\) --- the image
5772	  under the open immersion of the preimage of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is \(V\) again,
5773	  \(\iota\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\). Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
5774	  naturality squares for this conjugation commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\); this is
5775	  exactly the pattern already established for \(\mathtt{homLocalSection}\) and
5776	  \(\mathtt{dualUnitIsoGen}\). The forward and inverse maps are this conjugation and its
5777	  reverse, identifying the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom
5778	  value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.
5779	
5780	  % NOTE (review iter-261): this prose now lags the Lean route-2 build. In the Lean,
5781	  % `sliceDualTransport` packages BOTH legs (A and B) into one `LinearEquiv.toModuleIso`,
5782	  % not leg (A) alone; leg (A)'s forward component is built as a categorical
5783	  % `(ModuleCat.restrictScalars β_W).map (φ.app …)` (NOT an `eqToHom`-conjugation --- the
5784	  % `.map` form is load-bearing: a raw `ModuleCat.ofHom` reduces the carrier and loses the
5785	  % `restrictScalars`/`pushforward₀` module instance), and leg (B)'s `codomainMap` is the
5786	  % intended `inv (ε (restrictScalars β_W))`. A blueprint-writer should (i) retag this
5787	  % paragraph as the combined leg A∘B atom, (ii) replace "eqToHom-conjugation" with the
5788	  % `.map`/`inv ε` description, (iii) add a `\lean{...sliceDualTransport}` hint, and
5789	  % (iv) record the two open codomainMap frictions: (a) `CommRing` instance loss on the
5790	  % `forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat`-imaged section rings (blocks `restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective`),
5791	  % (b) the `𝟙_`-vs-`restr`-section defeq bridge needed for `exact inv (ε …)`.
5792	
5793	  The Step-4 residual is then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) of the sectionwise
5794	  composite of (leg (A), \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)) followed by (leg (B),
5795	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\) along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5796	  \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V).\mathrm{inv}\), \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}),
5797	  with the outer naturality thin-poset-trivial.
5798	
5799	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5800	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5801	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5802	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5803	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5804	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5805	
5806	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5807	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5808	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5809	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5810	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5811	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5812	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5813	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5814	
5815	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5816	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5817	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5818	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5819	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5820	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5821	
5822	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5823	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5824	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5825	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5826	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5827	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5828	
5829	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
5830	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
5831	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
5832	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
5833	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
5834	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
5835	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
5836	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
5837	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
5838	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
5839	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even

5873	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54

56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34

45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}

52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for

51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
54	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41

20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.

34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma

6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC

11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in

14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}

19	cohomology of an explicit complex built from the pushforwards of `F` over the
20	finite intersections of an affine open cover, producing an **unconditional**
